description: Seed patterns for the audit/memory-safety family. ripgrep is intentionally a shallow first pass here — memory-safety is the family where deeper sensors (CodeQL, Joern) matter most. These patterns surface allocation lifecycles, unsafe string operations, raw memory copy with potentially attacker-controlled sizes, and `unsafe` blocks in Rust. Each hit is a place a downstream skill should investigate by reading code, not a finding.
notes: C/C++ unsafe string functions (strcpy, strcat, sprintf, gets) are well-known footguns but not always exploitable; the skill must verify control over the source.
Bounded variants (strncpy, strncat, snprintf) are safer but still produce bugs around truncation, NUL handling, and size arithmetic.
memcpy/memmove are not unsafe per se — the question is whether the size argument is bounded by a checked invariant.
Rust `unsafe` blocks are not vulnerabilities by themselves; they are points where the human invariant must be verified.

c_cpp.false_positive_notes: Matches every allocation call; useful only as a frequency / proximity signal.
Common; useful to pair with malloc-family for lifecycle review.

c_cpp.description: Stack allocation with runtime size — classic stack-overflow vector if size is attacker-controlled
c_cpp.false_positive_notes: Rare but high-signal: any alloca with input-derived size deserves a closer look.

c_cpp.description: Unbounded C string operations — classic buffer overflow vectors
c_cpp.false_positive_notes: Almost always worth a look. False positives appear when source is a known fixed string.

c_cpp.description: Bounded C string operations — safer but still bug-prone (truncation, NUL handling, off-by-one)
c_cpp.false_positive_notes: Bounded does not mean safe. Check the size derivation.

c_cpp.description: Raw memory copy — usually safe unless size is attacker-controlled or off-by-one
c_cpp.false_positive_notes: High-frequency, low precision. Look at where the size argument comes from.

c_cpp.description: printf-family with a variable as the first argument — potential format-string vulnerability if the variable is attacker-controlled
c_cpp.false_positive_notes: Pattern is rough; fires whenever the format arg is an identifier rather than a string literal. Most cases are safe.

c_cpp.description: gets() — deprecated, removed in C11, always unsafe
c_cpp.false_positive_notes: If present at all, almost certainly a finding.

c_cpp.description: C++ new / delete — manual lifecycle, paired-call audit candidate
c_cpp.false_positive_notes: Will match `new T(...)` and `delete obj` constructs; some FPs in template / decltype contexts.
Almost always worth review when on network / file input.

c_cpp.description: C-style pointer cast to char*/uint8_t* — common around raw-buffer manipulation
c_cpp.false_positive_notes: Will fire in legitimate buffer-handling code. Used as a proximity signal.

c_cpp.description: malloc(n * size) — multiplication without overflow check is a common CVE pattern
c_cpp.false_positive_notes: Look at whether n and size are bounded. `calloc` is the safe variant for the same intent.

rust.description: Rust `unsafe { ... }` block — invariants that the compiler does not verify
rust.false_positive_notes: Not a vulnerability on its own. Investigate the invariant being asserted.

rust.description: Rust `unsafe fn` — function whose safety contract callers must uphold
rust.false_positive_notes: Same as unsafe block: marker, not a defect.

rust.description: std::mem::transmute — reinterpret bits as another type
rust.false_positive_notes: Always worth investigating; transmute is one of the most foot-gun-prone APIs.

rust.description: Pointer dereference of an `as *const T` / `as *mut T` cast
Box::from_raw / CString::from_raw / similar — manual ownership transfer
rust.false_positive_notes: Investigate who frees the pointer and when.

rust.description: Vec::set_len — unsafe API that bypasses initialization checks
rust.false_positive_notes: Almost always inside an unsafe block; verify the invariant.

go.description: Go unsafe package usage — pointer arithmetic / type punning
go.false_positive_notes: Go is mostly memory-safe; any unsafe.Pointer use is worth inspection.

go.description: Go cgo C-side memory primitives — boundary where Go's safety guarantees stop
go.false_positive_notes: Investigate ownership and lifecycle across the FFI.
